chunk_summary: Guidance on translating instrumental swallow test findings (VFSS/FEES) into actionable feeding prescriptions for neurodisabled patients, emphasizing safety, hydration, and participation.
learning_objectives: Understand how to interpret VFSS and FEES findings to create effective feeding prescriptions.
Learn to apply the Penetration-Aspiration Scale (PAS) for standardizing aspiration severity.
Identify key components of a report-to-prescription template for feeding support in neurodisability.
Recognize when repeat instrumental swallow testing is clinically justified.
key_facts: Pre-swallow aspiration often indicates poor oral control with premature spillage, commonly triggered by thin liquids.
Post-swallow aspiration is linked to residue spillover (vallecular or pyriform) and worsens with fatigue.
Prescriptions should specify IDDSI levels for liquids/solids, bolus volume, posture, pacing, and supervision requirements.
Fatigue testing is critical in neurodisability; emerging aspiration after repeated swallows necessitates time limits and breaks.
Repeat testing is justified only for major neurologic recovery, respiratory decline, or tube removal decisions.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Use the PAS to standardize severity and identify silent aspiration risk, but base decisions on the child’s overall physiology and response to strategies. (Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8721066/)
